perturbation.original_text: AGI hereby grants a non-exclusive license (the "License") to the Company and its operating subsidiaries now or hereafter existing to use the Good Sam name and logo, including trademarks, trade names, or service marks as designated by AGI (the "Trademarks"), in connection with the sale of such new and used vehicles by the Company that have satisfied such criteria and standards as are established from time to time by AGI (the "Approved Use"). AGI warrants that it has the right and ability to grant the License to the Company to use the Trademarks for the Approved Use in accordance with the terms of this Agreement and, provided that the Company uses the Trademarks for the Approved Use in accordance with the terms of this Agreement, such grant will not infringe upon the rights of any other party.
perturbation.changed_text: AGI hereby grants a non-exclusive right (the "License") to the Company and its operating subsidiaries now or hereafter existing to use the Good Sam name and logo, including trademarks, trade names, or service marks as designated by AGI (the "Trademarks"), in connection with the sale of such new and used vehicles by the Company that have satisfied such criteria and standards as are established from time to time by AGI (the "Approved Use"). AGI warrants that it has the right and ability to grant the License to the Company to use the Trademarks for the Approved Use in accordance with the terms of this Agreement and, provided that the Company uses the Trademarks for the Approved Use in accordance with the terms of this Agreement, such grant will not infringe upon the rights of any other party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'license' to 'right' implies that AGI is not granting a formal license but rather a permissive right to use their trademarks, even though the rest of the section refers to a 'License.' This creates ambiguity and could be interpreted as a failure to properly grant a trademark license.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act defines the requirements for granting a trademark license. By using the word 'right' instead of 'license,' and subsequently referencing 'License' later in the same paragraph, the contract introduces uncertainty as to whether a proper trademark license has been granted, potentially violating the Act's provisions regarding the valid transfer of trademark rights.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'license' to 'right' introduces ambiguity regarding whether a formal trademark license is granted. The Lanham Act, as suggested by the Law Explanation and implied by the snippets mentioning trademark registration and related procedures (e.g., sections on application for registration (1051), registrable trademarks (1052), and assignment (1060)), establishes specific requirements for valid trademark licenses. By using 'right' and then inconsistently referring to 'License,' the modified contract creates uncertainty about compliance with these requirements, potentially undermining the legal enforceability of the trademark usage agreement. This ambiguity directly contradicts the implied need for a clear and legally sound trademark license as supported by the law snippets referencing trademark rights and registration.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time in the event of a material breach by the other Party which remains uncured after thirty days written notice thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time in the event of a breach by the other Party which remains uncured after thirty days written notice thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'material,' any breach, no matter how small, could be grounds for termination. This contradicts the common legal understanding that only 'material' breaches justify contract termination, thereby creating uncertainty and potential for dispute.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1689 outlines the grounds for rescission of a contract. Removing the term 'material' broadens the grounds for termination beyond what is legally recognized, creating a clause that may be unenforceable under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Rescission [1688 - 1693] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1689. (a) A contract may be rescinded if all the parties thereto consent. (b) A party to a contract may rescind the contract in the following cases: (1) If the consent of the party rescinding, or of any party jointly contracting with him, was given by mistake, or obtained through duress, menace, fraud, or undue influence, exercised by or with the connivance of the party as to whom he rescinds, or of any other party to the contract jointly interested with such party. (2) If the consideration for the obligation of the rescinding party fails, in whole or in part, through the fault of the party as to whom he rescinds. (3) If the consideration for the obligation of the rescinding party becomes entirely void from any cause. (4) If the consideration for the obligation of the rescinding party, before it is rendered to him, fails in a material respect from any cause. (5) If the contract is unlawful for causes which do not appear in its terms or conditions, and
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the word 'material' from 'material breach,' which broadens the grounds for contract termination. California Civil Code § 1689(b)(4) allows rescission if 'the consideration for the obligation of the rescinding party, before it is rendered to him, fails in a material respect from any cause.' By removing 'material,' the contract now allows termination for any breach, no matter how small, which contradicts the established legal standard that only 'material' breaches justify termination. This undermines the legal intent and could render the clause unenforceable, since immaterial breaches wouldn't typically justify rescission under the law. The law snippet explicitly mentions 'material respect' as a condition for rescission, indicating that not all failures of consideration are grounds for termination.

perturbation.original_text: As consideration of Endorsement, concurrently with the execution of this Agreement, the Company shall pay to AGI a fee in the amount of $1,500,000. This fee shall be fully earned by AGI upon the receipt thereof and shall not be refundable for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: As consideration of Endorsement, concurrently with the execution of this Agreement, the Company shall pay to AGI a fee in the amount of $1,500,000. This fee shall be fully earned by AGI upon the receipt thereof and shall not be refundable, even in cases of gross negligence.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'even in cases of gross negligence' attempts to waive liability for gross negligence, which is against public policy and generally unenforceable. It suggests an attempt to shield AGI from responsibility even if they act with extreme carelessness.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts that have as their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. Adding language that seeks to avoid responsibility for gross negligence would likely be void as against public policy under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text adds 'even in cases of gross negligence,' which attempts to exempt AGI from responsibility for gross negligence. This directly contradicts California Civil Code § 1668, which states that contracts that exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against public policy. The addition weakens the original contract by attempting to waive liability, which the law explicitly prohibits. The scraped law snippet 1 directly states: 'All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law.'
